title: 第 48 節
en: He then asks for the knowledge of a sacrifice by which he may attain immortality in heaven, where WHAT DOES IT LEAD TO ? 347 there is no death, no old age, no hunger or thirst, and no sorrow. This too is granted.

And now it might be supposed that no more could be asked for. But Na/^iketas is not satisfied with the ordinary immortality in heaven, though it gives everlasting peace and happiness. He wants to know more and to have more, and says :
' There is that doubt, when a man is dead, some saying, he is ; others, he is not. This I should like to know, taught by thee ; this is the third of my boons.'

Then a fierce struggle begins between the young Brahman and Death. Death offers him anything rather than to tell him this mystery which, as he says, even the gods do not know. He offers him health and wealth, children and chariots, fair maidens and music, but all in vain. NaA^iketas will have his third boon, he will know what happens to man after he is dead, and Yama has at last to yield.
Then follows the answer of Yama or Death :
'Fools,' he says, 'dwelling in darkness, wise in their own conceit, and puffed up with vain knowledge, go round and round, staggering to and fro, like blind men, led by the blind.'

' The Hereafter never rises before the eyes of the careless child, deluded by the delusion of wealth. "This is the world," he thinks, " there is no other ; " and thus he falls again and again under my sway (the sway of death).' II. 5 seq.

Then follows the explanation of what constitutes the true being and the true immortality of man, what is called Atma in Sanskrit, and is generally translated by the soul, but which is better rendered by the self, the self that hes behind the Ego, behind the mere personality of man, and which is really, when fully known, the same as Brahman, the universal Self.
Yama continues :
' The knowing (Self) is not born, it dies not ; it sprang from 348 LECTURE XII.
nothing, nothing sprang from it. The ancient is unborn, eternal, everhisting, he is never killed, though the body is killed.' II. 18.

' The Self, smaller than small, greater than great, is hidden in the heart of the creature. A man who is free from desires and free from sorrow, sees the majesty of the Self by the grace of the Creator (or, through the tranquillity of the senses).' II. 20.
'That Self cannot be gained by the Veda, nor by understanding, nor by much learning. He whom the Self chooses, by him the Self can be gained. The Self chooses him as his own.' II. 23.
Then follows a parable which reminds one of a well-known passage in Plato's Phaedros :
' Know the Self to be sitting in the chariot, the body to be the chariot, the intellect (buddhi) the charioteer, and the mind the reins.'
' The senses they call the horses, the objects of the senses their roads. When he (the Highest Self) is in union with the body, the senses, and the mind, then wise people call him the Enjoyer.'
' He who has no understanding and whose mind (the reins) is never firmly held, his senses (horses) are unmanageable, like vicious horses of a charioteer.'
firmly held, his senses are under control, like good horses of a charioteer.'
' He who has no understanding, who is unmindful and always impure, never reaches that place, but enters into the round of births.'
' But he who has understanding, who is mindful and always pure, reaches indeed that place, from whence he is not born again.' III. 3-8.

' Kise, awake ! having obtained your boons, understand them ! The sharp edge of a razor is difficult to pass over ; and thus the wise say that the path (that leads to the Self, or to Self-knowledge) is hard.' III. 14.

' He who has perceived that which is without sound, without touch, without form, without decay, without taste, eternal, without smell, without beginning, without end, beyond the Great, and unchangeable, is freed from the jaws of death.' III. 15.

Here ends the first chapter. Then follows a second which contains a collection of verses, all bearing on our subject, but full of allusions to minute points of Indian philosophy. It would require more time than we can spare to make their real meaning quite clear, WHAT DOES IT LEAD TO ? 349 and I must be satisfied with quoting a few of the more simple and telling verses :
' No mortal lives by the breath that goes up and by the breath that goes down. We live by another, in whom these two repose.' V. 5.

' He, the Highest Person, who is awake in us while we are asleep, shaping one lovely sight after another, that indeed is the Bright, that is Brahman, that alone is called the Immortal. All words are contained in it, and no one goes beyond. This is that.'

' As the one fire, after it has entered the world, though one, becomes different according to whatever it burns, thus the one Self within all things becomes different, according to whatever it enters, and exists also without.' V. 9.

' As the sun, the eye of the whole world, is not contaminated by the external impurities seen by the eyes, thus the one Self within all things is never contaminated by the misery of the world, being himself without.'
' There is one ruler, the Self within all things, who makes the one form manifold. The wise who perceive him within their Self, to them belongs eternal happiness, not to others.'

'There is one eternal thinker, thinking non-eternal thoughts, who, though one, fulfils the desires of many. The wise who perceive him within their Self, to them belongs eternal peace, not to others.' V. 11-13.
zh: 他隨後詢求一種祭祀的知識，藉此得以在天界中獲得不朽，在那裡沒有死亡、沒有衰老、沒有飢餓或乾渴，也沒有悲傷。這也被賜予了。
現在看起來似乎沒有什麼更可要求的了。但納基塔（Nákiketas）對於天堂裡的普通不朽並不滿足，儘管它能帶來永恆的安寧與幸福。他想了解更多、擁有更多，因此說道：
「有這樣的疑惑，當人死亡時，有人說他存在；有人說他不存在。我想要知道這個，由你來教導我；這是我第三個祈願。」
於是年輕的婆羅門與死神之間展開激烈的鬥爭。死神寧願獻出任何東西，也不願洩露這個祕密——正如他所言，連眾神都不知曉。他獻上健康與財富、子嗣與戰車、美麗的少女與音樂，但一切都徒勞無功。那基塔（Naaiketas）執意要得到他的第三個願望，他要知道人死後會發生什麼，閻摩王終於不得不屈服。
隨後是閻摩（Yama）或死亡之神的回答：
他說：「愚人住在黑暗中，自以為聰慧，被虛妄的知識所充滿，如盲人被盲人引領一般，搖搖晃晃地團團轉。」
「來世永不會呈現在被財富幻象所迷惑的粗心孩童眼前。他想著『這就是世界，沒有別的世界；』於是他一次又一次地陷入我的統治之下（死亡的統治）。」二‧五及以下。
接著是對人的真實存在與真實不朽性之本質的說明，這在梵文中稱為「阿特曼」（Atma），通常被譯為「靈魂」，但更恰當的譯法是「自我」——那隱藏於自我意識之後、隱藏於人之單純人格性之後的自我，而這個自我在充分認識時，實際上與梵（Brahman）——宇宙的自我——無二無別。
夜摩繼續說：
「知者（自我）既不生，亦不死；它源自
無，無從中生起。古者未生，永恆，永存，雖身體被殺，然彼永不被殺。』II‧18。
「自我，比細微者更細微，比宏大者更宏大，隱藏在眾生的心中。一個人若從慾望中解脫，從悲哀中解脫，便能藉由造物主的恩典（或者，通過感官的寧靜）看見自我的尊威。」II. 20.
「自我（Self）不能由吠陀所得，亦不能由理解所得，亦不能由多聞所得。自我所選擇者，自我即為其所得。自我選擇他作為其自身。」II. 23.
接著是一個比喻，它使人想起柏拉圖《斐德羅篇》(Phaedros) 中一段眾所周知的段落：
「知自我坐於車中，身體為車，智慧（buddhi）為御者，心為韁繩。」
「感官他們稱為馬匹，感官的對象乃其道路。當他（最高自我）與身體、感官和心意相結合時，智者稱他為享受者。」
他若無知慧，其心（韁繩）未嘗堅固，其諸感官（馬匹）難以駕馭，如車夫之烈馬。
堅定不移，其感官如馭者之良馬般受到控制。
「那些沒有悟性、心思不寧且常不清淨者，永遠不能到達那個境界，反而陷入輪迴之中。」
「但具有理解力者，心念專一且常保清淨，確實會到達那個地方，從此不再輪迴出生。」III. 3-8.
「起來吧！既然獲得了你的恩賜，就要領悟它們！剃刀的銳利邊緣是難以通過的；聖賢們因此說，通往自我（或自我知識）的道路是艱難的。」III. 14.
「誰若已體悟那無聲、無觸、無形、無衰、無味、永恆、無嗅、無始、無終、超越偉大、不可變之物者，即得免於死亡之頜。」《奧義書》第三卷，第十五章。
第一章至此終結。隨後是第二章，內含一組詩節，全都涉及我們的主題，但充滿了對印度哲學細微之處的暗示。要使其真實含義完全明晰，需要花費超過我們所能撥出的時間，我必須滿足於引述其中較為簡明且有力的若干詩節：
「沒有任何凡人靠著上升的呼吸與下降的呼吸而活著。我們靠著另一位而活著，這兩種呼吸在他身上得到安息。」V. 5.
「他，最高的人格，當我們沉睡時他卻保持清醒，一個接著一個地塑造出美麗的景象，那確實是光明，那就是梵（Brahman），那單獨被稱為不朽者。一切言語都包含於其中，無人超越於外。這就是那個。」
第8章
「如同單一的火，進入世界後，雖然本為一體，卻因所燃燒之物而各異；同樣，存在於萬物之中的單一自我（Self），因進入不同之物而有所不同，亦存在於萬物之外。」第九章。
如同太陽——整個世界的眼睛——不為眼睛所見的外在污穢所染污，同樣地，存在於一切事物中的那個唯一自我（the one Self）也永遠不為世界的苦難所染污，因為他本身超越於外。
「有一位統治者，他是萬物內的自我，使唯一的形式變為眾多。智者在自身內覺知他，永恆的幸福便屬於他們，而非屬於他人。」
「有一位永恆的思想者，思考著非永恆的思想，他雖然唯一，卻滿足眾多者的欲望。智者在其自身內感知他，對他們而言才有永恆的和平，對他人則無。」第11–13節。
